name: Asclepius Extended
description: Asclepius, in Greek mythology, is the god of medicine, healing, rejuvenation, and physicians. His origins are shrouded in myth, with various accounts claiming him as the son of Apollo and either Coronis, Arsinoe, or Erato. He was trained in the art of medicine by the centaur Chiron, mastering not only surgery and herbal remedies but also the secrets of immortality. Asclepius's healing abilities were so potent that he could even raise the dead, a feat that angered Zeus, who feared the disruption of the natural order and the balance of power between gods and mortals. As punishment, Zeus struck Asclepius down with a thunderbolt. However, Apollo, in grief, retaliated by killing the Cyclopes who had forged the thunderbolt. Asclepius is often depicted with a serpent-entwined staff, the Rod of Asclepius, which remains a prominent symbol of medicine today. His cult centers were located at Epidaurus, Kos, and Trikala, where supplicants would seek healing through incubation, a process of sleeping in the temple in hopes of receiving divine guidance in their dreams. Asclepius's influence extended throughout the ancient world, and he was revered as a benevolent and powerful deity, bringing solace and hope to the sick and suffering. His legacy continues to resonate in modern medicine, with the principles of healing and care deeply rooted in his mythological origins. He is also associated with snakes, considered sacred creatures, and dogs, who were believed to lick wounds and aid in healing. He had numerous children, including Hygieia (goddess of health, cleanliness, and hygiene), Panacea (goddess of remedies), Iaso (goddess of healing), Aceso (goddess of the healing process), and Machaon and Podalirius, who were physicians in the Trojan War.
subtitle: God of Medicine and Healing

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Asclepius
keyMyths.description: Asclepius's birth is shrouded in mystery, with some accounts claiming his mother Coronis was killed by Artemis for infidelity to Apollo, while others state she simply died. Apollo rescued the unborn Asclepius from her womb.

keyMyths.title: Asclepius's Healing Powers
keyMyths.description: Trained by Chiron, Asclepius became an unparalleled healer, capable of curing the sick and even raising the dead, which challenged the natural order.

keyMyths.title: Zeus's Punishment
keyMyths.description: Zeus, fearing Asclepius's power to defy death, struck him down with a thunderbolt, an act that sparked Apollo's wrath and led to the killing of the Cyclopes.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive summary of Greek mythology.
